topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. "Please note that Professor Albright will be stepping down from their position at the university, effective the end of the semester." My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk to keep them from flying open. A strange, buzzing sensation started behind my eyes, making the fluorescent lights of the library seem to strobe. I reread the sentence, then the signature, then reread it again. It didn't make sense. The world seemed to tilt, colors bleeding at the edges. I pushed my chair back so hard it scraped across the linoleum floor. A choked sound escaped my throat, and I clamped a hand over my mouth, trying to force the sound back down, but it kept bubbling out—a high, thin noise. I needed air. I scrambled to my feet, my legs feeling like water, and bolted from the study room, the sound of my own frantic breathing drowning out everything else.

[story 2]

I stared at the notification, my vision blurring into streaks of grey and white. Albright. Gone. The words swam before my eyes, mocking the stability I had so carefully constructed around this project. My chest tightened, a sudden, crushing pressure that made drawing a full breath an impossible task. I paced the small confines of my apartment, my footsteps echoing hollowly on the wooden floor. Every surface seemed too bright, too sharp. I began pacing in tight circles, my jaw clenched so hard my teeth ached. "No," I whispered, the word raspy and thin. I ran both hands through my hair, pulling sharply, feeling the strands tug painfully. A hot flush spread across my neck, and I pressed my palms against my temples, trying to physically contain the sudden, overwhelming surge of agitation that threatened to unravel me completely.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the official correspondence, the ink seemed to vibrate. Professor Albright was leaving. The reality of it hit me with the force of a physical impact, knocking the air from my lungs. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, a rapid, fine tremor that made holding my pen feel like an impossible feat. I sank onto the edge of my bed, curling inward, trying to make myself small enough to disappear. A wave of intense, restless energy washed over me, making my skin prickle as if I were submerged in static. I started muttering to myself, a rapid-fire stream of nonsensical questions, my voice pitched too high. "What happens now? What do I do? It's all falling apart." I stood up abruptly, knocking over a stack of textbooks, and stared at the scattered pages as if they held the answers I desperately needed, though they only seemed to emphasize the emptiness surrounding me.
